coredata.dc:title: Physicochemical properties of in situ microwave-assisted germanium oxide modified nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Machumele, Khanani P.
Mokoloko, Lerato L.
Makatini, Maya M.
Maubane-Nkadimeng, Manoko S.
coredata.dc:description: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with germanium oxide (GeO2/GeO) using a facile one-step microwave-assisted synthesis procedure is presented. Iso-ascorbic acid, urea and Ge-132 were used as carbon, nitrogen and germanium sources, respectively. Studies have shown that the CDs, NCDs and GeO2/GeO nanoparticles individually have excellent qualities for bio-imaging applications including photoluminescence, biocompatibility, good stability and low toxicity. While CDs have been thoroughly explored in literature, CDs with germanium oxide on their surface are an avenue that has scarce information. Hence the synthesis and physicochemical characterizations of CDs- /NCDs- GeO2/GeO composites were studied using different microscopic and spectroscopic techniques to explore their compatibility for bio-imaging applications. In the presence of Ge, the approximate particle sizes of both the CDs and NCDs increased from 2.5 nm to 5.8 nm and the spherical or quasi-spherical shape was maintained. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy survey spectrum showed that the surface of Iso-N-Ge-CDs contained nitrogen and germanium, indicating the successful synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with germanium oxide (GeO2/GeO). Photoluminescence emissions of longer wavelengths suitable for cell imaging were observed for Iso-N-Ge-CDs (350 nm) and Iso-Ge-CDs (430 nm) and the Iso-N-Ge-CDs are able to fluoresce under the red and blue wavelengths. The Ge-NCDs demonstrated excitation-dependent emission wavelength behaviour, pH sensitivity and Fe3+ sensitivity which may be used as a probe for Fe3+ metal ions and intracellular pH measurements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Germanium
Photoluminescence
Wavelength excitation-dependence
Bio-imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Research Foundation of South Africa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of South Africa (Grant numbers: 138075, 138081) and The South African Medical Research Council. We acknowledge the Wits Mass Spectrometry Division and the Wits Microscopy and Microanalysis Unit for the spectroscopy and microscopy data respectively. We also wish to thank Lindokuhle Magagula for the data discussions.

This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of South Africa (Grant numbers: 138075 , 138081 ) and The South African Medical Research Council . We acknowledge the Wits Mass Spectrometry Division and the Wits Microscopy and Microanalysis Unit for the spectroscopy and microscopy data respectively. We also wish to thank Lindokuhle Magagula for the data discussions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs and (inserted) size distribution curves of (a) Iso-CDs, (b) Iso-Ge-CDs, (c) Iso-N-CDs and (d) Iso-N-Ge-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of (a) Iso-CDs, (b) Iso-N-CDs, (c) Iso-N-CDs and (d) Iso-N-Ge-CDs.
(a) Survey scans of Iso-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs showing four major peaks of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and germanium, and the expanded survey scan of (b) C1s (c) N1s and (d) Ge2p3.
XRD spectra of the various CDs.
Photoluminescence (PL) of (a) Iso-CDs (b) Iso-Ge-CDs (c) Iso-N-CDs d) Iso-N-Ge-CDs.
Photoluminescence spectra of the Iso-N-Ge-CDs at pH ranging from 1 to 11.

(a) The emission peak intensity ratio of Iso-N-Ge-CDs with Cu2+, Fe3+, Co3+, Zn3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Na+, Al3+, K+, As3 +and As2+ ions, (b) Photoluminescence (PL) of carbon dots of Iso-N-Ge-CDs with different metal ions, (c) fluorescent properties of the different metal ions under UV-light and (d) a concentration study of the Fe3+ metal ion.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of Iso-CDs, Iso-Ge-CDs, Iso-N-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs.
Fluorescent Microscopy of Iso-N-Ge-CDs under blue (a) and red (b) wavelength.
A simple illustration of the fabrication process for the various CDs and Ge-CDs composites presented in this work.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: “–” = this precursor was not used for that particular experiment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Data derived from the Photoluminescence data.

head.ce:title: Physicochemical properties of in situ microwave-assisted germanium oxide modified nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with germanium oxide (GeO2/GeO) using a facile one-step microwave-assisted synthesis procedure is presented. Iso-ascorbic acid, urea and Ge-132 were used as carbon, nitrogen and germanium sources, respectively. Studies have shown that the CDs, NCDs and GeO2/GeO nanoparticles individually have excellent qualities for bio-imaging applications including photoluminescence, biocompatibility, good stability and low toxicity. While CDs have been thoroughly explored in literature, CDs with germanium oxide on their surface are an avenue that has scarce information. Hence the synthesis and physicochemical characterizations of CDs- /NCDs- GeO2/GeO composites were studied using different microscopic and spectroscopic techniques to explore their compatibility for bio-imaging applications. In the presence of Ge, the approximate particle sizes of both the CDs and NCDs increased from 2.5 nm to 5.8 nm and the spherical or quasi-spherical shape was maintained. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy survey spectrum showed that the surface of Iso-N-Ge-CDs contained nitrogen and germanium, indicating the successful synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with germanium oxide (GeO2/GeO). Photoluminescence emissions of longer wavelengths suitable for cell imaging were observed for Iso-N-Ge-CDs (350 nm) and Iso-Ge-CDs (430 nm) and the Iso-N-Ge-CDs are able to fluoresce under the red and blue wavelengths. The Ge-NCDs demonstrated excitation-dependent emission wavelength behaviour, pH sensitivity and Fe3+ sensitivity which may be used as a probe for Fe3+ metal ions and intracellular pH measurements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Additional data to support the findings is provided in Supplementary information

ce:section.ce:para: Traditionally, carbon dots (CDs) are classified as nanoparticles that are generally spherical or quasi-spherical possessing a diameter that is 10 nm or smaller [1–3]. There are four classes of CDs known thus far, these are carbonized polymer dots, carbon nanodots, graphene quantum dots, and carbon quantum dots [4,5]. CDs are considered the most promising allotrope of carbon for drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensors, tissue engineering and cancer therapy. This is due to their versatile physicochemical properties including good water solubility, superior quantum yields, low cytotoxicity, favourable biocompatibility, good photostability, high sensitivity, great cell membrane permeability, tuneable fluorescence emission and excitation, and selectivity for target analytes [6,7]. These qualities make them far better candidates for aforementioned biomedical applications compared to other carbon allotropes and metal-based quantum dots. For example, to activate the fluorescent qualities of carbon nanotubes and graphene, further modification of their structures and surface chemistry is required, whereas the as-prepared CDs already possess these fluorescent qualities without any modification. On the other hand, metal-based quantum dots are traditionally synthesized from heavy metals that may be toxic, however, CDs have shown to overcome these limitations [8]. Additionally, the synthesis of CDs is facile and can be fabricated using low energy, and a broad variety of carbon sources can be exploited including waste such as banana peels, peanut shell and rose petal, carbohydrates, polymers and biomass [8]. As such, there has been an exponential growth in the number of CD publications in biomedical applications over the past few years [9].

Another important quality of the CDs that makes them attractive is the fact that they allow structure and interface modification which enhance their features for a specific application. This is done by doping the carbon lattice with foreign atoms such as nitrogen. This has shown significant improvement in the electrical dispersion and appropriate electronic energy levels of CDs and thereby improving their fluorescent and other features [10]. For example, Wang et al. synthesized CDs plus nitrogen-doped CDs (or NCDs) with blue fluorescence, and utilized them to enhance the light-harvesting qualities during the plant's photosynthesis process [11]. The authors reported that the NCDs had better light conversion and electron donor features which contributed to the promotion of the photoelectron transfer rate. Liao et al. synthesized NCDs with a high fluorescence and higher quantum yield (28.46 %) compared to undoped CDs (quantum yield = 5.3 %) [12]. Additionally, the NCDs showed quick and extreme sensitivity while also having a selective fluorescence quenching effect toward Au3+ ions.

Composites of CDs with metal atoms also form nanomaterials with attractive features for various applications [13]. For example, Lou et al., were able to produce a highly efficient photocatalyst for hydrogen generation using Pt-NCDs [14]. The catalyst synthesis strategies also showed great potential for electrocatalysis, heterogeneous catalysis and drug delivery applications. In another study, Safardoust-Hojaghan and Salavati-Niasari prepared composites of NCDs with TiO2 and showed that by introducing the NCDs nanoparticles, photodegradation efficacy of TiO2 to methylene blue under UV light (400 W UV tube) is increased from 40 % to 85 % [15]. Li et al., showed that Mg- and N-codoped CDs have enhanced photoluminescence traits compared to CDs alone [16]. Furthermore, biocompatibility, superior photostability and reduced cytotoxicity are some of the properties of the Mg- and N-codoped CDs which showed great potential for bioimaging applications. Indeed, studies show that there has been a growing interest in these metal-CDs nanocomposites, especially in clean energy research [17].

Other interesting composites of CDs and metal to study for biomedical applications are the CDs and germanium (Ge)/germanium oxide (GeO2/GeO) composites. This is because both CDs and Ge have excellent features for biomedical applications. Like the CDs, Ge nanoparticles also possess quantum confinement, a broader optical range and powerful visible photoluminescence [18]. Germanium is a member of group 4 elements. It is a non-metals similar to C and Si, and it is believed that it may feasibly have sp3-like bonding which may also impact the electronic and structural features of carbon-based material, but in a unique way compared with other dopants such as N or S [19]. Germanium is a semi-metal that is mostly used as a semiconductor. Elemental germanium is similar in appearance to pure silicon, and it readily forms complexes when reacted with oxygen. A trace amount of germanium can be found naturally in food such as broccoli, celery, garlic and ginseng but it is not an essential mineral [20–23]. Germanium oxide nanoparticle is also reported to have both photoluminescent as well as antimicrobial traits [24,25]. Anticancer activity of synthetic organo‑germanium compounds such as bis-carboxyethyl germanium sesquioxide or Ge132, has several biological applications and is reported in numerous cancer cell models and clinical trials due to their antioxidant features [26]. Other widely exploited metal-containing anticancer pharmaceuticals including cisplatin may be circumvented by organogermanium compounds such as Ge-132 that enhance pro-inflammatory responses by boosting interferon-γ (IFN-γ), natural killer cell and T-cell activity, and are substantially less harmful. Therefore, great synergy and enhanced photoluminescence, higher biostability, higher activity and selectivity can be expected. This could potentially broaden the application of both CDs and Ge.

Only a few journal articles have reported on the synthesis of CDs-Ge/GeO2 composites. However according to literature they are biocompatible and have low toxicity. Yuan et al. reported that the Ge-CDs composite for the detection of mercury ions in cancer cells which demonstrated that bio-application is feasible for these compounds [27]. The composite exhibited a blue-green luminescence, excitation-dependent photoluminescence traits, as well as a high sensitivity toward mercury ions. Another study by Yu-Cing Juho et al. evaluated the improvement of blood circulation and sexual satisfaction using a unique FIR fiber called Ge-Ti-textile made of germanium, titanium, and various polymers [28]. After three months of therapy, the Ge-Ti-underpants treatment considerably raised the average penile blood flow velocity and enhanced sexual function, according to the findings. On the other hand, in a different study, the activity of composites of CDs-Ge-GeO2 was investigated for the photocatalytic degradation of pendimethalin and dinotefuran pesticides [29]. It was found that the CDs-Ge-GeO2 can degrade ∼100 % of both pendimethalin and dinotefuran pesticides under visible light illumination (>440 nm) in <30 min. This high photocatalytic degradation was ascribed to the synergy between the composite of the components. To enlarge the scope of these CDs-Ge/GeO2 composites in practical applications, this study reports on a simple microwave assisted reaction for the synthesis of germanium oxide-modified NCDs. Ge-NCDs produced were fluorescent, water-soluble, and emitted different colours due to the different sizes. To the best of our knowledge, the effect of both germanium and nitrogen on the photoluminescent traits of CDs has not been reported. We found that the CDs modified with germanium oxide have photoluminescent traits which are crucial for bio-imaging and are sensitive toward Fe3+.

Iso-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), urea (CH₄N₂O) and bis (2-carboxyethyl germanium (IV) sesquioxide) (Ge-132) were all purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Merck), South Africa, and used as received. Distilled water and hydrochloric acid (HCl 32 %, Sigma Aldrich, South Africa) were utilized as solvents in the experiments.

Carbon dots (CDs) and carbon dots composites were synthesized using previously reported isoascorbic acid and urea-derived CDs technique but with modifications [30,31]. The schematic representation of the CDs preparation is summarized in Scheme 1 below. The experiments were carried out in a CEM Discovery SP microwave operating under closed vessel conditions at a maximum power and pressure output of 150 W and 250 psi, respectively. Before the reaction, the precursors were dispersed in 8.0 mL distilled water and 2 mL 0.1 M HCl until a homogenous mixture was formed. Thereafter, the mixture was transferred into a 30 mL microwave reaction vessel, placed in the microwave oven, and irradiated at 150 W for 5 min until an infrared temperature of 125 °C was reached. After the reaction, it was observed that the reaction mixture changed colour from clear to black-brown, which symbolized carbonization [30]. The reaction vessel was allowed to cool down to room temperature before the acid was neutralized by adding 0.1 M NaOH. The resulting solution was purified by centrifugation at 14000 rpm for 20 min to separate the supernatant. The supernatant was then filtered using a hydrophilic 0.22 μm nylon syringe filter membrane. Finally, a rotary evaporator was then employed to evaporate the water from the CDs solution. The obtained product was allowed to dry in an oven at 100 °C overnight. The quantity of precursors used in each reaction as well as the product yields are summarized in Table 1 below. The products were named Iso-CDs, Iso-Ge-CDs, Iso-N-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs depending on the precursors utilized in each experiment, as explained in Table 1.

The morphology of the synthesized CDs and modified CDs was probed using FEI Technai G2 Spirit transmission electron microscope operated at 120 kV. A Tecnai G2 30ST high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) operating at 200 kV was used for HRTEM images. The particle size distribution was analysed using the ImageJ software [32]. A Bruker D2 phaser with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å) was employed to determine the powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of the samples. The data was collected from 10° to 70° 2θ. The Agilent Technologies Cary Series UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used, acquiring data using an absorption spectrum with a range from 200 to 800 nm and varying at an excitation wavelength of 245 nm to 288 nm. The Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy data were obtained from Varian Cary Eclipse EL04103870 fluorescence spectrophotometer at a range between 320 and 380 nm, by exciting the sample solutions at 300 nm to 400 nm wavelength. The analysis of the functional groups on the CDs was explored using a Bruker Tensor 27 FTIR analyser. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was collected using a quantum 2000 scanning ESCAlab 250Xi microprobe system operated at 300 W with monochromatic Al kα (1486.7 eV). The fluorescent images were captured using the Olympus BX63 Fluorescence Microscope (OFM). The processing of the captured images was conducted on Fiji ImageJ software.

CDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted technique with commonly used precursors for CDs synthesis such as isoascorbic acid as a carbon source and urea for nitrogen doping [31]. However, for the reactions reported here, HCl was used as one of the solvents to help accelerate the formation of the CDs during the reaction [33]. Typically, microwave-assisted irradiation of ascorbic acid produces CDs with approximate average particle sizes of 2 to 3 nm at microwave powers between 600 and 900 W for ≥5 min [30,31]. Herein, it is shown that CDs from isoascorbic acid, with average sizes of 3.2 ± 1.2 nm and an average size ranging between 2.0 and 8.0 nm (Fig. 1a) and the corresponding Ge-modified (Fig. 1b), can be produced under reduced temperature, power and reaction time by simply adding HCl to the reaction mixture. Likewise, nitrogen-doped CDs synthesized from equal amounts of iso ascorbic acid and urea, when irradiated at 600 W for 6 min were produced with particle sizes of ∼4 nm [34]. In this work, the average particle sizes were 2.2 ± 1.88 nm, with an approximate size range between 1.0 nm and 5.5 nm as seen in Fig. 1c. Other studies have shown that machine-learning-based techniques can be used to further fine-tune the microwave-assisted reaction for optimum CDs productions (i.e., production of CDs with high % yield, quantum yield and other properties) [35].

The incorporation of germanium oxide (GeO2/GeO) led to a slight enlargement in the CDs particle sizes as shown by the size distribution graphs in Fig. 1b) and d). For example, the Iso-Ge-N-CD's approximate average particle sizes were 5.8 ± 0.78 nm Fig. 1d), which is ∼3 nm larger than the Iso-N-CDs (i.e., without GeO2/GeO modification). The enlargement in the CDs particle sizes may be ascribed to the large sizes of Ge atomic radius (211 pm) on the carbon lattices or surface functionalization, which will be further discussed in the XPS analysis. The quasi-spherical morphology of the CDs did not change when germanium was incorporated in both Iso-Ge-CDs and Iso-Ge-N-CDs. High-resolution TEM images were taken to determine the lattice fringes of the prepared samples (if any), and the results obtained from each sample are inserted on images in Fig. 1. The lattice d-spacing of Iso-CDs was found to be 0.18 nm corresponding to the (004) place of a graphitic structure (insert Fig. 1a and supplementary information Fig. S1) [36]. Surprisingly, the Iso-Ge-CDs sample did not show any lattices even at higher resolution (insert Fig. 1b and S2), which implies that these nanoparticles are highly amorphous compared to the other samples. Iso-N-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs had lattice d-spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane lattice of graphite as seen in insert Fig. 1c, d and Figs. S3 and S4 [37]. The d-spacing of the CDs were consistent with the literature [38], the slight changes in size may be attributed to doping with nitrogen and germanium. Moreover, in order to comprehend structural morphology, quantify qualities, measure particle size distributions, validate findings, and conduct comparative analysis [39], a number of TEM images were obtained for the CDs and are presented in Figs. S5 – S8 for Iso-CDs, Iso-Ge-CDs, Iso-N-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs respectively.

Fig. 2 below displays the FT-IR spectra of Iso-CDs, Iso-Ge-CDs, Iso-N-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs. The absorption peaks of all the samples showed a broad intense absorption band at approximately 3247 cm−1 indicating the existence of –O–H or O–H/N–H for N-doped CDs. The peak at approximately 2939 cm−1 was due to the CH stretching vibrations and the peak at approximately 1747 cm−1 and 1606 cm−1 can be ascribed to CO stretching vibrations and CC stretching vibrations, respectively. The characteristic absorption band of CN stretching at approximately 1392 cm−1 and the absorption band at approximately 1053 cm−1 is ascribed to C–O–C stretching vibrations. Interestingly, the Ge-containing samples showed a significant reduction in the CC bands, which could imply that the incorporation of Ge somehow reduces the graphitic nature of the CDs.

XPS analysis was further conducted to study the surface elemental analysis of the doped and Ge-modified CDs. The XPS survey spectrum comparison of Iso-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs shown in Fig. 3a) clearly shows the carbon (C1s) and oxygen (O1s) surface functionalities of Iso-CDs (in black), versus the Iso-N-Ge-CDs (in blue) which further contains nitrogen (N1s) and germanium (Ge2p3), consistent with other co-doped carbon dots [40]. The Iso-N-Ge-CDs expanded XPS spectra of the C1s (Fig. 3b) showed peaks at 284.7, 286.0, 287.9 and 288.7 eV, matching the sp2 CC and/or NC, sp3 C-C/N–C, CO, and O–C=O and/or N–C=O bonds, respectively [34]. The O1s peak deconvolution (Fig. S9) revealed the presence of OC as well as the O–C=O and/or N–C=O bonds at binding energies of 531.5 and 532.7 eV, respectively. N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) revealed the presence of graphitic-N (399.6 eV) and NOx (401.3 eV) [34]. Finally, the Ge2p3 spectrum (Fig. 3d) showed a peak at 1219.6 eV, which is evident in the presence of GeO and GeO [41]. This data suggest that nitrogen was successfully incorporated into the carbon lattice as proven by the presence of graphitic-N, however, the absence of a GeC bond suggests that there were no bonds formed between the core of CDs and germanium. The XPS data of Iso-CDs (Fig. S10), Iso-Ge-CDs (Fig. S11) and Iso-Ge-CDs (Fig. S12) showed the expanded survey scan consistent with literature [27,41].

The atomic abundance of carbon in Iso-CDs, Iso-Ge-CDs, Iso-N-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs, was found to be 64.6 %, 63.4 %, 58.9 % and 55.4 % respectively (Table S1, S2, S3, and S4). This data illustrated that when nitrogen or germanium is incorporated into the sample, the abundance of carbon is reduced. As a result, Iso-N-Ge-CDs, which contain both nitrogen and germanium, have the lowest carbon content. The relative abundance of C1s, O1s, N1s and Ge2p3 for Iso-N-Ge-CDs were estimated to be 55.4, 25.9, 15.9 and 2.8 w%, respectively Table S4. Additionally, incorporating nitrogen affected carbon's atomic abundance more significantly. This may be attributed to the fact that during doping, nitrogen replaces some of the graphitic carbons in addition to the surface functional groups, as seen in the breakdown of C, N, O species found in Iso-N-CDs (Table S5) and species breakdown of C, N, O and Ge in Iso-N-Ge-CDs (Table S6) found in the supplementary information. The comparison of the atomic species between Iso-CDs and Iso-Ge-CDs seen in the supplementary information Table S7 and S8, respectively, demonstrated that germanium affected the surface functional groups only since no GeC bonds were detected. Interestingly, the CDs containing nitrogen (Iso-N-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs) had a higher CO content compared to the CDs which did not contain nitrogen (Iso-CDs and Iso-Ge-CDs). This may be due to the fact that urea (the nitrogen source) is also known to be a reducing agent [42]. Carboxyl groups (O=C–OH) can undergo reduction reactions to form various reduced products, depending on the reaction conditions and the nature of the reducing agent used. This suggests that urea further reduced carboxylic groups to form functional groups such as alcohols, esters, and aldehydes, which increased the CO content in the samples.

The PXRD pattern (Fig. 4 ) of the synthesized CDs showed one broad peak centred at 2θ of 17°, 20°, 23° and 22° for Iso-CDs, Iso-Ge-CDs, Iso-N-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs, respectively. The peaks that appear at these 2θ ranges are typically observed for amorphous carbon [43,44]. The incorporation of nitrogen and (especially) germanium resulted in a significant broadening of the peak for Iso-N-CDs, Iso-Ge-CDs and Iso-N-Ge-CDs, which implied that the incorporation of heteroatoms in the CDs structure makes them more amorphous. Interestingly, Iso-Ge-CDs appear to produce the broadest peak, indicating that it is more amorphous compared to the other CDs [45]. Moreover, this highly amorphous nature correlates to the lack of lattice fringes previously observed in the high-resolution TEM.

CDs have distinct optical characteristics that make them ideal for photoluminescence (PL) research. Additional investigations, such as PL excitation mapping, PL decay dynamics, or PL imaging, might be investigated depending on the unique research objectives and applications. For example a study by Pyng Yu and co-workers investigated the temperature-dependent fluorescence measurements on highly fluorescent CDs between 77 and 300 K [46]. Their data indicated that CDs have metallic-nanocluster-like temperature behaviour. Herein, the excitation dependence, pH sensitivity and quenching capacity were investigated using photoluminescence spectroscopy.

The CDs showed very intriguing luminescent qualities. Fig. 5 demonstrates the PL spectra of the newly prepared 5 μg/mL CDs aqueous solutions. Each PL spectrum revealed a single band, indicating the origin of luminescence should be the same. A red shift was observed in the spectrum for all the CDs while increasing the excitation wavelength [47]. The maximum emission (and full width at half maximum) was observed for (a) Iso-CDs (b) Iso-Ge-CDs (c) Iso-N-CDs (d) Iso-N-Ge-CDs an excitation of 330 nm (95 nm), 340 nm (110 nm), 330 nm (120 nm) and 330 nm (90 nm), respectively, as seen in Table 2 . Moreover, the defects that are present in the material and the inhomogeneity of the material are known to affect the full width at half maximum (FWHM) which may give more insight regarding the fabricated samples [48]. Literature has shown that when the material is doped and the maximum emission as well as the FWHM in PL is affected [38], this can be ascribed to the changes in the width of the absorption line due to the lattice vibrations and lattice imperfections [49]. The intensity of the peaks appears to be low compared to literature, however, their ability to emit photons after the excitation and recombination processes demonstrated that they do possess photoluminescent properties [50]. The photoluminescence and quantum yield are closely related as they are both linked to the efficiency of the light emission process [51].

Interestingly, PL emission with a longer wavelength is more favourable because the cell nuclei generally emit blue light. Hence, the CDs presented in this work are more suitable for cell imaging applications [52].

CDs PL characteristics can be greatly impacted by N-doping. CDs can have their electrical and surface properties changed via N-doping, which entails adding nitrogen atoms to the carbon lattice. CDs photoluminescence efficiency can be increased by N-doping, which can also cause their emission spectra to shift toward the red. Although the exact mechanism underlying the PL enhancement in N-doped carbon dots is unknown, it is thought to be connected to the entry of new energy levels into the bandgap of the CDs as illustrated in Fig. S13. When nitrogen atoms are added, N-doped graphitic domains can emerge, which can produce new energy levels inside the CD bandgap. This could explain why the intensity of Figs. 5 (a) and (b) appears to be lower than the nitrogen-doped CDs in Figs. 5 (c) and (d).

Since the CD concentrations were kept constant, the intensities were compared and the Iso-N-Ge-CDs exhibited the best photoluminescent properties. This sample was then selected for the pH and quenching study. The surface charge, size, and chemical makeup of CDs can all be influenced by the pH, which can have an impact on how they behave [53].The protonation or deprotonation of surface functional groups, changes in the electronic structure of CDs, and changes in the local microenvironment of the CD are just a few examples of the many variables that can affect the PL properties of carbon dots [54]. Emission wavelength, intensity, and quantum yield, change as a function of pH CDs may be used in bioimaging, sensing, and medication delivery, all of which depend on pH for optimal functioning [55].

Several studies have shown that C-dots with pH-sensitive PL show tremendous potential for monitoring pH changes in living cells, tissues, and animals, demonstrating that they can be used as a useful research tool in biomedical studies. For instance, CDs can be utilized as pH sensors in bioimaging because of their pH-dependent photoluminescence characteristic. Liu's group created two fluorescent CDs with pH-sensitive red fluorescence in the pH range of 1.0 to 9.0 and a linear range of 3.5 to 6.5. This was shown to be useful for measuring the pH value in living cells MCF-7 cells and were used to test the ability of CDs to monitor intracellular pH fluctuations [56].

Fig. 6 shows the pH-sensitive properties of Iso-N-Ge-CDs. The maximum excitation is observed at a pH of 3, and the minimum is pH 11. Interestingly, there appears to be a linear increase from pH 4 to 6. Since tumor tissues have an acidic extracellular pH of 6.2–6.8, and Iso-N-Ge-CDs seem to have stronger photoluminescent properties at low pH, then this suggests that the Iso-N-Ge-CDs possess good prospects for monitoring pH changes in living cells or releasing pH sensitive cargo [57].

Carbon dots can interact with ion metals due to their great surface area and variety of surface functional groups. Ion metals and carbon dots may interact, which may change some characteristics, including photoluminescence, stability, and toxicity which can be used for sensing [35]. Comprehending the mechanism of interaction between carbon dots and ion metals can be aided by ion metal investigations. For instance, ion metals can snuff out CDs photoluminescence, which can be exploited for sensing purposes. The photoluminescence of carbon dots, on the other hand, can be enhanced by ion metals and employed for bioimaging and sensing applications. For example, the human body cannot usually metabolize Fe3+ ions and too much of them can lead to cancerous cell growth [58]. As a result, it is crucial to find Fe3+ ions. With an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the quenching capabilities of 12 different metal ions (0.01 mM) were tested to clarify the fluorescence sensing of 1 mg/mL CDs for Fe3+ ions. This study was evaluated by adding 0.5 mL of the CD solution and 0.5 mL of the metal ions at room temperature. The emission peak intensity of Iso-N-Ge-CDs with different metal ions, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Co3+, Zn3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Na+, Al3+, K+, As3 +and As2+ ions, was depicted in Fig. 7 (a-b). According to the findings, when compared to other metal ions, Fe3+ ions had the highest level of quenching for Iso-N-Ge-CDs.

In Fig. 7 (c), the samples containing the metal ions were placed under a UV lamp, and the data reveal that the photoluminescence spectroscopy matched the fluorescent intensity seen and UV light. The concentration of the Fe3+ was then varied from 0.01 mM to 0.09 mM, and 5 μg/mL was used for the study in Fig. 7 (d). A consistent decrease was detected as the concentration of the Fe3+ was increased. The quenching effect of Fe3+ can be reversible, and in a similar study by Yan Liang et al., an “on-off-on” photoluminescent properties using glutathione (GSH) of Fe 3+ was demonstrated. [56]

According to prior studies, the intramolecular photoinduced electron transfer process from excited CDs to Fe3+ ions is a plausible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of CDs [57]. The d-orbital of Fe3+ splits when electron-deficient Fe3+ complexes containing functional groups on the surface of the N-CDs (such as carboxyl groups and amino groups) are added as shown in Fig. S14. As a result, a portion of the electrons in the excited state of the CDs were transported to the d-orbital of the Fe3+. Fluorescence quenching resulted from the radiation forms restrained electron transition (FL emission).

The absorption spectra of Iso-CDs, Iso-Ge-CDs, Iso-N-CDs, and Iso-N-Ge-CDs are shown in Fig. 8 below. Iso-CDs showed absorption peaks at ∼204 and 291 nm, which correlated to the π → π* and n → π* transition of CC and CO bonds, respectively. The absorption peaks for Iso-Ge-CDs were observed at ∼204 and 267 nm, correlating to the π → π*, characteristic of the aromatic sp2 domains. Iso-N-CDs showed peaks at ∼214 and 243 nm, correlating to the π → π* transition of the CC bond. Finally, similar to the Iso-Ge-CDs, Iso-N-Ge-CDs absorbed UV–Vis radiation at ∼204 and 264 nm, correlating to the π → π*, characteristic of the aromatic sp2 domains. The absorption edges of all four samples extend into the visible band. The products were dispersed in water and were exposed to UV light and each exhibited greenish luminescence (Fig. S15).

The BX63 Upright Microscope was then utilized to analyse the fluorescent qualities of the Iso-N-Ge-CDs to determine their capacity for bio-imaging applications. A single grain of the Iso-N-Ge-CD powder was placed on a glass slide and analysed under the microscope, and this revealed that the Iso-N-Ge-CD can fluoresce under the red and blue wavelengths, Fig. 9 . Most CDs fluoresce in the blue only however the Iso-N-Ge-CD fluoresces in longer wavelength regions as well. CDs which are red display high resistance to photobleaching, intense absorption in the red/NIR region, and biodegradability [58].

A successful synthesis of CDs (diameter ≤ 10 nm) using a simple microwave assisted method was confirmed using TEM. The FTIR spectra showed that the CDs were successfully doped with nitrogen for the Iso-N-Ge-CDs, as shown by the characteristic band at approximately 3247 cm−1 (NH) and 1392 cm−1 (CN) and further confirmed by the XPS data with the relative abundance of N1s estimated to be 15.9 w %. The presence of GeO2/GeO on the carbon dots was also confirmed by the XPS data however, the lack of CGe interaction suggests that the incorporation of germanium only affected the surface functionalities. The effects were more notable in the size increase of the CDs shown in TEM as well as the significant shift and broadening of the peaks on the XRD. The photoluminescent qualities of the CDs show that the CDs are fluorescent, and when exposed to a UV light, the CDs exhibited a wide range of colours. PL data also showed that the CDs have photoluminescent qualities. Data from the BX63 Upright Microscope showed that the Iso-N-Ge-CDs exhibit fluorescence, which can be applied for bio-imaging applications. The Iso-N-Ge-CDs demonstrated the best photoluminescent capabilities, although for future work, further studies may be conducted to improve these properties. The linear rise in pH from 4 to 6 in the pH-sensitivity study may be exploited because tumor tissues have an acidic extracellular pH of 6.2–6.8. This implies that Iso-N-Ge-CDs have promising applications for monitoring pH changes. Moreover, the quenching study of the different metal ions revealed that Fe3+ ions showed the highest quenching effect for Iso-N-Ge-CDs.

Khanani P. Machumele (Conceptualisation, writing, formal analysis), Manoko S. Maubane-Nkadimeng (Conceptualisation, formal analysis, editing, supervision); Maya M. Makatini (Conceptualisation, formal analysis, editing, supervision), Lerato L. Mokoloko (Formal Analysis, Editing).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of South Africa (Grant numbers: 138075, 138081) and The South African Medical Research Council. We acknowledge the Wits Mass Spectrometry Division and the Wits Microscopy and Microanalysis Unit for the spectroscopy and microscopy data respectively. We also wish to thank Lindokuhle Magagula for the data discussions.
